content.full_text:
CITY OF OAKLAND

Nov 15 10:24 AM '91

CITY HALL • ONE CITY HALL PLAZA, • OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612
(415) 273-3601
Office of the City Attorney                                                TTY 839-6451
Jayne W. Williams
City Attorney                                    November 14, 1991


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J. Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca. 95804

                Re:  Request for Advice, Government Code section 83114(b)
                     Oakland Hills Fire

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

                          I.  INTRODUCTION

     I am requesting expedited advice on behalf of 20 City of
Oakland elected and appointed officials and employees as identified
in Section IV of this letter.  This request for expedited advice
is needed as a result of the October 20, 1991 Oakland Hills fire,
which damaged or destroyed approximately 3,526 housing units of
which approximately 3,027 were single family residences.1/  The
fire area is comprised of approximately 1,900 acres or three square
miles.  In excess of 5,000 persons were displaced as a result of
the fire.2/  The fire area encompassed real property in two Oakland
City Council Districts.  The fire crossed jurisdictional boundaries
and also destroyed houses in Berkeley3/  and burned acreage in
Piedmont.  As a result of the conflagration, the City of Oakland
was declared a national disaster by President Bush, and a state of
emergency was proclaimed by Governor Pete Wilson.

     The 20 elected and appointed officials and the employees
each have significant functions or responsibilities related to fire

_____________________
     1  Figures released by the American Red Cross as of November
8, 1991.  These figures are subject to revision as differing
figures have been released from other sources.

     2  Figures estimated by City of Oakland Planning Department
based on 1990 census block maps.

     3  Approximately 60 houses were destroyed in Berkeley.  This
figure is based on anecdotal information.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
November 14, 1991
Page 2

recovery which could have a material effect on their financial
interests because of their ownership of, or residence in properties
within the generalized fire area or within 2,500 feet of it.

I have provided you with two maps prepared by the City of
Oakland Planning Department; one which shows the fire area, the
other the generalized fire area and both show the location of the
20 City of Oakland public officials' real property in relation to
these areas.2/

As you are well aware, conflict of interest not only
applies to making or participating in making a decision, but also
to influencing a decision. All of the individuals named in this
letter are at least in a position to influence and to participate
in the making of decisions relating to fire recovery. Our elected
and appointed officials will make final decisions relating to fire
recovery. The expedited response is needed because fire recovery
activities have been in progress since October 21, 1991, the day
after the fire, and are ongoing.

4 In Map One which is a parcel map, the properties within the
solid black lines represent destroyed properties. The shaded areas
within the solid black lines represent properties which did not
burn. The area within the solid black lines is the "fire area".
The dashed line represents a 300 foot perimeter around the fire
area while the dashed and dotted line represents a 2,500 foot
perimeter around the fire area. The area within the red line is
the "generalized fire area". The generalized fire area represents
the "No Entry Zone" which was established by the Oakland Fire
Department at 4:00 p.m. on Monday, October 21, 1991, plus the
eastern portions of the Claremont Country Club, St. Mary's Cemetery
and Mountain View Cemetery. Map Two is a street map, which
contains one solid black circle representing the generalized fire
area. The solid black circle in Map Two includes properties which
did not burn. Map Two more accurately encompasses neighborhoods
which will be affected by fire recovery decisions since on Map One,
only parts of streets were included in the fire area and it is more
realistic that a fire recovery activity will affect the entire
street or entire neighborhood, not just a segment of it. Also fire
recovery decisions could impact the Claremont Country Club, St.
Mary's Cemetery and Mountain View Cemetery since properties of each
of these entities were affected by the fire. The 300 foot and
2,500 foot perimeters represented by a dashed line and a dashed and
dotted line, respectively, in Map Two, are measured from the edge
of the generalized fire area.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
November 14, 1991
Page 3


II. ISSUE PRESENTED

Decisions made by the 20 City of Oakland public officials
who reside or own property within the generalized fire area or
within 2,500 feet thereof should be examined for conflict of
interest under the "Public Generally" exception to the conflict of
interest laws because the effect of decisions on their financial
interests will be substantially the same as the effect on
significant segment of the public. (Cal. Admin. Code tit. 2
§18703.)

III. LEGAL ANALYSIS

The City Council meets weekly, the Planning Commission
every other week and each of these bodies anticipates fire related
items on their agenda at every meeting well into the distant
future. In addition, the City Councilmember and the Planning
Commissioners are members of task forces related to fire recovery
and participate or will participate in workshops regarding the
same. Some of the employees named herein are involved in hourly
committee meetings in which they develop strategies for fire
recovery. Some of these employees may process loans for fire
victims and others will inspect the property of fire victims for
code compliance. It will be nearly impossible to apply the FPPC's
real property regulation using the 300 and 2,500 foot rule because
of the size and boundaries of the fire area. (Cal. Admin. Code,
tit. 2 §18702.35 subd. (a) and (b).) Even if one uses section
18702.3 subdivision (c) for decisions which do not involve a
subject property from which distances can be determined, it will be
difficult if not impossible to dissect each of these public
official's activities which will occur at a rapid nonstop pace to
determine if any of these activities will affect the public
official's real property value by $10,000. Of course, if fire
recovery does not occur, everybody in Oakland loses, property tax
revenues would decrease and the property value of the surrounding
remaining properties including those in other jurisdictions will
decline.

The conflict of interest laws were created to prevent a
public official from having an unfair advantage by virtue of that
official's position. In the Ferraro opinion, Commissioner
Remcho's concurring opinion spoke of a highly publicized rent
control ordinance and that such publicity would act as a deterrent
for otherwise interested Councilmembers from engaging in self
serving decisions even though they owned rental properties. (4
FPPC Ops. 62, pp. 75-78 (1978).) The public scrutiny is even
greater in the City of Oakland as the impact of a rent control
ordinance in Los Angeles pales in comparison to fire recovery
activities which will dictate how and when the 5,000 plus residents

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
November 14, 1991
Page 4


of the fire zone can rebuild their destroyed properties. It has
been more than three weeks since the fire and not a day has passed
without multiple articles in the local newspapers. The City of
Oakland's response to this disaster is not only being scrutinized
by the media, but by each and every affected resident and other
interested parties.

The regulations interpreting the conflict of interest
laws take into consideration factors such as the impact of a
decision on a significant segment of the public and allow public
officials to participate in the decision making process even when
the decision could have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on the officials' interest, so long as that decision affects
the public generally, in substantially the same manner. (Cal.
Admin. Code tit. 2 §18703.) The "Public Generally" exception does
not just apply to the total population of a jurisdiction, it can
apply to a significant segment of the public. Past FPPC opinions
and advice have indicated that "significant segment of the public"
must be large in number and heterogeneous in population. It is
obvious that the fire affected a significant and varied segment of
the public. Clearly, the affected property owners are not
representative of any particular interest group, the only thing
they have in common is that they all owned property which was
destroyed by fire or was in imminent danger of being destroyed by
fire. However, past advice and opinions from the FPPC have not
applied an arithmetic formula to this exception. Such a formula
becomes unwieldy and inappropriate in a national disaster. Past
formulas from the FPPC have gone as low as 1% for significant
segment of the public, but applying that to Oakland would require
that over 17,000 housing units burn before such a formula could
apply. Approximately 2,48 of all of Oakland's housing units were
burned in the fire; approximately 5% of all single family homes
were burned and the fire area comprised approximately 5% of
Oakland's land. Despite the level of destruction, unless adjusted,
the arithmetic formula used by the FPPC in the past would not allow
for City of Oakland public officials named herein to participate in
the decision making process regarding fire recovery.

In the Ferraro opinion, Commissioner Remcho noted in
his concurring opinion that disqualification disenfranchises the
constituency of the disqualified official and stated that,
"although the immediate impact of disqualification is felt by the
official rather than by the voters, it is the voters who go
unrepresented." (4 FPPC Ops. 62 p. 11 (1978).)

Here, the constitutional issue could hardly be more
acute. It would be extremely constitutionally suspect to
disqualify the disaster victims' elected representative solely
because she too lived in the fire area. To do so would be to deny

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
November 14, 1991
Page 5


those hurt most by the disaster from appropriate representation in
the City's rebuilding efforts.

IV.  DUTIES OF THE NAMED PUBLIC OFFICIALS AND LOCATION OF THEIR
REAL PROPERTY⁵/

     1.    Oakland City Councilmember Marge Gibson-Haskell, an
elected official who represents Oakland City Council District
Number One and whose district encompasses a great majority of the
fire area, owned two single family residences both of which were
destroyed by the fire. She resided in one of the dwellings and
rented out the other. (E-1 and E-2)

     The types of fire recovery decisions that the
Councilmember will make which could have a material effect on her
financial interest include:

     whether to amend the general plan, subdivision
regulations and other local environmental regulations;

     whether to amend building, fire, zoning, housing or other
related codes to require construction with fire resistant
materials including a ban on shake roofs; to create
greenbelts and open space;

     whether to declare public necessity to acquire property
by eminent domain for street widening;

     whether to create special assessment districts to finance
improvements such as sewers, street lighting and
undergrounding;

     developing policies regarding revegetation of the burned
area including restrictions on certain types of plants;

     participating on a task force which includes other
elected officials to provide input to City staff and the
Oakland City Council on fire rebuilding solutions; and


_____________________
     ⁵ The location of the public officials' real property on the
attached maps is indicated by a letter followed by a dash and a
number in parentheses.  This key corresponds with both maps.
Public officials represented on both maps as B-4, E-3, F-4, F-5, F-
6 and B-3 do not reside or own property within the generalized fire
area or within 2,500 feet of it so they are not identified in this
letter.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
November 14, 1991
Page 6

hearing appeals from the Planning Commission regarding
fire recovery.

2.     Oakland Planning Commissioner Joseph DeLuca resides
within the generalized fire area and is three blocks away from
Mountain View Cemetery which was affected by the fire and four
blocks away from houses which burned.     (D-1)

3.     Oakland Planning Commissioner Peter Smith resides
within the generalized fire area.  Houses across the street from
him and on the block behind his street were destroyed but his house
was spared. (D-2)

     The Planning Commissioners are nominated by the Mayor of
the City of Oakland and confirmed by a majority vote of the City
Council.  The Commissioners can be anticipated to discuss
rebuilding solutions in the context of amendments to the zoning
regulations, including:

          rezoning properties if necessary to create green belts
          and open space;

          amendments to the general plan, subdivision regulations,
          and;

          local environmental regulations.

     The Commissioners may also make recommendations to the
City Council regarding the need for legislative changes to state
and federal environmental regulations. In addition, fire and panic
safety regulations as they relate to land use and planning will be
discussed.  The Commissioners will also be involved in a multitude
of permit appeals and public hearings for major permits and making
environmental determinations.

     4.     Craig Kocian, Assistant City Manager, resides within
2,500 feet of the generalized fire area. (C-7) The City Manager's
Office oversees all city of Oakland departments with the exception
of the Office of the Mayor, the City Auditor's Office and the
Office of the City Attorney.  The City Manager's Office will be
involved in all aspects of fire recovery including monitoring
legislation that will impact the fire victims, drafting and
entering into contracts for cleanup, rebuilding streets, sewers and
undergrounding.

     5.     Ramon Rodriguez, a Rehabilitation Supervisor in the
Office of Community Development, resides within the generalized
fire area and is two houses away from houses which were destroyed.
(A-8) His duties related to fire recovery could include assembling

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
November 14, 1991
Page 7

packages for rehabilitation loans if the City of Oakland becomes
involved in providing such loans for work in the fire zone. This
work would include assessment of work to be done and specifications
for the contracts. The Supervisor also reviews and recommends
approval of assessment of work to be done and specifications.

6. Teresa Sevilla, a Rehabilitation Advisor in the
Office of Community Development, resides within the generalized
fire area and resides two blocks away from houses which were
destroyed. ((A-10) Her duties related to fire recovery could
include assembling packages for rehabilitation loans for
rehabilitation work in the fire area. This work would include
assessment of work to be done and specifications for the contracts.

7. Lutgarda Obando, a Rehabilitation Advisor in the
Office of Community Development, resides in Piedmont but within
2,500 feet of the perimeter of the generalized fire area. (G-1)
Her duties related to fire recovery could include assembling
packages for rehabilitation loans if the City of Oakland becomes
involved in providing rehabilitation loans for rehabilitation work
in the fire area. This work includes assessment of work to be done
and specifications for the contracts.

8. Gilbert Presto, a Mortgage Advisor in the Office of
Community Development, lost his home in the fire. (G-2) His
position is also in the Rehabilitation section. Mortgage Advisors
compile the loan packages for rehabilitation loans. If the Office
of Community Development becomes involved in rehabilitation loans
for the fire area, Mortgage Advisors will compile the loan
packages.

9. John Stewart, a Housing Inspector in the Office of
Community Development, lost his home in the fire. (A-11) His
duties related to fire recovery could include identifying hazardous
situations such as chimneys or parts of buildings in danger of
collapse in the fire area. To date, Mr. Stewart has not been
involved in these activities.

10. William Singman, a Housing Inspector in the Office
of Community Development, resides within 2,500 feet of the
perimeter of the generalized fire area. (B-5) His duties related
to fire recovery include identifying hazardous situations such as
chimneys or parts of buildings in danger of collapse in the fire
area. The information is provided to Public Works which is
preparing an inventory for demolition and clean up. Some Housing
Inspectors have also begun to identify habitable properties to
determine if conditions are such that people may return to their
homes.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
November 14, 1991
Page 8

11. Ronald Ward, Engineer in the Public Works
Department, resides within the generalized fire area. (C-11) His
house is four blocks away from houses that burned. His duties
related to fire recovery could include inspecting sewers for
compliance with City of Oakland Codes.

12. Jon Ingenthron, the City's Risk Manager, lost his
home in the fire. (A-5) His duties related to fire recovery
include advising City of Oakland fire recovery staff on insurance
issues and analyzing the risk of proposed decisions regarding fire
recovery.

13. Glen Tepke, a Budget Analyst for the City Manager's
Office, resides within the generalized fire area. (D-3) His house
is next to burned houses. His duties related to fire recovery
include fiscal analysis of revenue lost with respect to the fire.

14. Terry Roberts, the Director of Public Works, resides
within 2,500 feet of the perimeter of the generalized fire area.
(C-12) His duties related to fire recovery include leading the
department which inspects properties for imminent hazards and
implements abatement. The department has established a
comprehensive cleanup operation, and an erosion control plan which
will provide mitigation for windblown dust and ash, potential
landslides, debris flows, flooding, mud flows, and transport of
debris and sediment onto downstream property and water. This
department will ensure that the building permit process is
streamlined to service homeowners in the burn area to meet the goal
of rebuilding the community in an expeditious manner. It will
process building permits and it will ensure that the infrastructure
in the burn area is evaluated to determine integrity and assess the
need to repair. It will ensure that debris is cleared from the
public rights of way and it will implement infrastructure repairs.

15. Jay Musante, an Urban Analyst for the Office of
Economic Development and Employment, resides within 2,500 feet of
the generalized fire area. (B-7) His duties related to fire
recovery include assisting in staffing at 5354 Claremont Avenue,
Oakland, also known as the Community Assistance Center (CAC). At
the CAC information and referrals are provided to displaced in-home
businesses and displaced workers. He will also market FEMA/SBA
economic injury assistance to impacted neighborhood businesses.

16. Joyce Hicks, Assistant City Attorney, resides in
Piedmont but within 2,500 feet of the generalized fire area. (C-3)
Her duties related to fire recovery include supervising attorneys
who provide legal advice to the City Council, boards, commissions
and all City departments on all aspects of fire recovery, from
creating special assessment districts to amending fire, building,

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
November 14, 1991
Page 9


zoning, housing and other municipal codes.

17. Marilyn Kaplan, Assistant to the City Attorney,
resides in Berkeley but within the generalized fire area. (F-2)
All the houses behind her house burned. Her duties relating to
fire recovery could include defending the City against lawsuits
related to the fire.

18. Jannie Wong, Assistant to the City Attorney, lost
her home in the fire. (F-3) Her duties relating to fire recovery
could include defending the city against lawsuits related to the
fire.

19. Ines Vargas Fraenkel, Assistant to the City
Attorney, resides within the generalized fire area. (F-1) Houses
two blocks away from her house burned. Her duties relating to fire
recovery could include defending the City against lawsuits related
to the fire.

20. Robert Graham, Assistant to the City Attorney,
resides within the generalized fire area. (F-4) Houses four
blocks away from his house burned. His duties relating to fire
recovery could include defending the City against lawsuits related
to the fire. He currently serves on a City committee regarding
fire related insurance issues.

CONCLUSION

For the foregoing reasons, the "Public Generally"
exception should apply to City of Oakland public officials engaging
in fire recovery activities who reside or have an interest in real
property in the generalized fire area. I would greatly appreciate
an expedited response so that I can advise the affected public
officials.

Very truly yours,

Jayne W. Williams
City Attorney

Attorney Assigned:
Joyce M.Hicks
Enclosures

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Decisions made by the 20 city of oakland public officials
who reside or own property within the generalized fire area or
uithin 2,500 feet thereof should be exarnined for conflict 
of
interest under the tiPublic cenerally exception to the confLict of
interest laws because the effect of decisions on their financial
interests will 
be substantially the sane as the effect 
on
significant 
segTment of the public. 
(CaI. Adnin. Code tit. 
2
s18703. )
III. 
LEGAIJ ANAIJYSIS
The City Council meets weekly, the Planning Cornmission
every other week and each of these bodies anticipates fire related
items on their agenda at every meeting well into the distant
future. In addition, the City Councilmenber and the Planning
Cornmissioners are nenbers of task forces related to fire recovery
and participate or will participate in $rorkshops regarding the
sarne. Sone of the enployees named herein are involved in hourly
cornmj,ttee meetings in vhich they develop strategies for fire
recovery. 
Sone of these employees may process loans for fire
victims and others will inspect the property of fire victims for
code conpli.ance. It will be nearly impossible to apply the FPPC.s
real property regulation using the 30o and 2,500 foot rule because
of the size and boundaries of the fire area. (Ca1. Adnin. Code,
tit. 
2 518702.35 subd. (a) and (b).) 
Even if one uses section
18702.3 subdivision (c) for decisions which do not involve a
subject property fron which distances can be deterrnined, it will be
difficult 
if not impossible to dissect each of these public
officialrs 
activities 
whj.ch will occur at a rapid nonstop pace to
determine whether any of these activities will affect the public
officialrs 
real property value by $10,000. Of course, if fire
recovery does not occur, everybody in oakland loses, property tax
revenues rrould decrease and the property value of the surrounding
renaining properties including those in other jurisdictions will
decline.
The confLict of interest Laws were created to prevent a
public official 
frorn having an unfair advantage by virtue of that
official rs position. 
In the Ferraro opinion, 
Conruissioner
Renchors concurring opinion spoke of a highly publicized rent
control ordinance and that such publicity would act as a deterrent
for otherwise interested councilmembers from engaginq in self
serving decisions even though they owned rental properties. 
(4
FPPC Ops. 62, pp. 75-7A (1978).) 
The public scrutiny is even
greater in the city of Oakland as the inpact of a rent control
ordinance in Los Angeles pales in cornparison to fire recovery
activities which will dictate hol,, and when the 5,000 plus residents


ltr. scott Hallabrin
November 14, 19 91
of the fire zone can rebuild their destroyed properties. 
It has
been more than three weeks since the fire and not a day has passed
without nultiple articles in the local newspapers. The City of
Oaklandrs response to this disaster j-s not only being scrutinized
by the Dedia, but by each and every affected resident and other
interested parties .
The regulations interpreting the confLict of interest
Iaws take into consideration factors such as the impact of a
decision on a significant segrnent of the public and allow public
officials to participate in the decision making process even when
the decision coul,d have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on the officials' 
interest, so long as that decision affects
the public generally, in substantially the same manner. (CaI.
Admin. Code tit. 
2 S18703.) The "Public cenerallyrr exception does
not just apply to the total population of a jurisdiction, 
it 
can
apply to a significant segment of the public, 
Past FPPC opinions
and advice have indicated that t'significant segrnent of the publicrr
nust be large in nunber and heterogeneous in population. It is
obvious that the fire affected a significant and varied segment of
the public. 
C1ear1y, the affected property owners are not
representative of any particular interest group, the only thing
they have in common is that they all owned property which was
destroyed by fire or s/as in imminent danger of being destroyed by
fire. 
Hovrever, past advice letters and opinions frorn the FPPC have
applied an arithmetic formula to this exception. Such a formula
becones unwieldy and inappropriate in a national disaster. Past
fornulas fron the FPPC have gone as Iow as ]-2Z for significant
segment of the public, but applying that to oakland would require
that over 17,000 housing units burn before such a formula couLd
apply. Approxinately 2.4* of aII of Oaklandts housing units lrere
burned in the fire; approxinately 5t of all single farnily hones
were burned and the fire area comprised approxinately 5t of
Oaklandrs land. Despite the level of destruction, unless adjusted,
the arithmetic formula used by the FpPc in the past would not allow
for City of oakland public officiafs 
named herein to participate in
the decision naking process regarding fire recovery.
In the Ferraro opinion, Commissioner Remcho noted
in his concurring opinion that disqual ification disenfranchises the
constituency of the disqualified 
official 
and stated that,
rralthough the inmediate impact of disqua I if ication is felt by the
official 
rather than by the voters, it is the voters who go
unrepresented. rr (4 FPPC Ops. 62 p. 11, (1978).)
Here, the constitutional issue could hardly be more
acute. 
It 
would be extrenely constitut ional ly suspect to
disqualify the disaster victimsr elected representative sole1y
because she too lived in the fire area. To do so would be to deny


u!. Scott Hallabrin
November L4, L99L
those hurt nost by the disaster fron appropriate representation in
the city's rebuilding efforts.
rv.
1. 
oaklandl City Councilmember l,targe cLbsoD-Eastell, an
elected official 
who represents Oakland City Council District
Number One and whose district 
encompasses a great najority of the
fire area, owned two single farnily residences both of which were
destroyed by the fire. 
She resided in one of the dlrellings and
rented out the other. 
(E-1 and E-2)
The types of 
fire 
recovery decisions that 
the

financial interest i.nclude:
rrhether to arnend the general plan, subdivision

related codes to require construction with fire resistant
materj.als including a ban on shake roofs; to create
greenbelts and open space i

by erninent domain for street widening;
whether to create special assessment districts to finance
irnprovements such as seqrers, street lightinq 
and
undergrounding;
developing policies regarding revegetation of the burned
area including restrictions on certain types of plants;
participating on a task force which includes other
eLected officials to provide input to City staff and the
Oakland City Councj.L on fire rebuilding solutions; and

embedding.first_500_words: CITY OF OAKLAND Nov 15 10:24 AM '91 CITY HALL • ONE CITY HALL PLAZA, • OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612 (415) 273-3601 Office of the City Attorney TTY 839-6451 Jayne W. Williams City Attorney November 14, 1991 Mr. Scott Hallabrin Counsel, Legal Division Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J. Street, Suite 800 P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, Ca. 95804 Re: Request for Advice, Government Code section 83114(b) Oakland Hills Fire Dear Mr. Hallabrin: I. INTRODUCTION I am requesting expedited advice on behalf of 20 City of Oakland elected and appointed officials and employees as identified in Section IV of this letter. This request for expedited advice is needed as a result of the October 20, 1991 Oakland Hills fire, which damaged or destroyed approximately 3,526 housing units of which approximately 3,027 were single family residences.1/ The fire area is comprised of approximately 1,900 acres or three square miles. In excess of 5,000 persons were displaced as a result of the fire.2/ The fire area encompassed real property in two Oakland City Council Districts. The fire crossed jurisdictional boundaries and also destroyed houses in Berkeley3/ and burned acreage in Piedmont. As a result of the conflagration, the City of Oakland was declared a national disaster by President Bush, and a state of emergency was proclaimed by Governor Pete Wilson. The 20 elected and appointed officials and the employees each have significant functions or responsibilities related to fire _____________________ 1 Figures released by the American Red Cross as of November 8, 1991. These figures are subject to revision as differing figures have been released from other sources. 2 Figures estimated by City of Oakland Planning Department based on 1990 census block maps. 3 Approximately 60 houses were destroyed in Berkeley. This figure is based on anecdotal information. Mr. Scott Hallabrin November 14, 1991 Page 2 recovery which could have a material effect on their financial interests because of their ownership of, or residence in properties within the generalized fire area or within 2,500 feet of it. I have provided you with two maps prepared by the City of Oakland Planning Department; one which shows the fire area, the other the generalized fire area and both show the location of the 20 City of Oakland public officials' real property in relation to these areas.2/ As you are well aware, conflict of interest not only applies to making or participating in making a decision, but also to influencing a decision. All of the individuals named in this letter are at least in a position to influence and to participate in the making of decisions relating to fire recovery. Our elected and appointed officials will make final decisions relating to fire recovery. The expedited response is needed because fire recovery activities have been in progress since October 21, 1991, the day after the fire, and are ongoing. 4 In Map One which is a parcel map, the properties within the solid black lines represent destroyed properties. The shaded areas within the solid black lines represent properties which
